coredata.dc:title: Gelatin-based packaging material incorporated with potato skins carbon dots as functional filler
coredata.dc:creator: Min, Seungjae
Ezati, Parya
Rhim, Jong-Whan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CD) were developed via hydrothermal synthesis using potato peel as functional fillers for food packaging films. The CD showed strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activity with low cytotoxicity as more than 80% of mouse fibroblast L929 cells survived even when exposed to 500 μg/mL for 72 h. The CD was used as a functional filler for preparing a gelatin-based functional film, and it was uniformly dispersed in biopolymer, indicating a compatible packaging film. The FTIR result showed no change in the chemical structure, but an H-bond was apparent between the CD and gelatin. The addition of CD produced a highly transparent film with significant improvement in the water vapor permeability (by 28%) and hydrophobicity (by 9% and 13%) properties of the gelatin film without much changing of the mechanical properties. The CD-added gelatin films indicated UV-barrier property and strong antioxidant activity. Moreover, the antibacterial efficacy increased significantly. Therefore, the CD-added gelatin films have great potential as a value-added, low-cost, safe, and functional nanofiller for the preparation of packaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Antioxidant
Antimicrobial
Functional packaging film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FTIR and XRD patterns of films
Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA) of films
The release rate of carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CD: (a) TEM images, (b) particle distribution size, (c) UV–vis light absorption and color of CD solution under visible and UV light, (d) XRD, and (e) FTIR spectra.

Cell viability and optical microscopy images of mouse fibroblast L929 cells after 72 h treatment with various potato skin carbon dots (CD) concentrations. (a): control, (b): 0.1, (c): 0.25, (d): 0.5, and (e): 1.0 mg/mL.
(a) Apparent photograph and (b) surface and (c) cross-sectional FE-SEM images of the gelatin-based films.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of the gelatin-based films.
Release rate of CD from the gelatin-based films.
(a) Effect of CD concentration on antioxidant activity. (b) Antioxidant activity of the gelatin-based films. Different letters within the same column indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).
Antibacterial activity of CD determined by the agar well-diffusion and disk-diffusion methods.
Antimicrobial activity of the gelatin-based films.
Mechanical properties, WVP, and WCA of the gelatin-based films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The values are presented as a mean ± standard deviation. Different letters within the same column indicate significant differences (p < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Gelatin-based packaging material incorporated with potato skins carbon dots as functional filler

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, Seoul 02447, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CD) were developed via hydrothermal synthesis using potato peel as functional fillers for food packaging films. The CD showed strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activity with low cytotoxicity as more than 80% of mouse fibroblast L929 cells survived even when exposed to 500 μg/mL for 72 h. The CD was used as a functional filler for preparing a gelatin-based functional film, and it was uniformly dispersed in biopolymer, indicating a compatible packaging film. The FTIR result showed no change in the chemical structure, but an H-bond was apparent between the CD and gelatin. The addition of CD produced a highly transparent film with significant improvement in the water vapor permeability (by 28%) and hydrophobicity (by 9% and 13%) properties of the gelatin film without much changing of the mechanical properties. The CD-added gelatin films indicated UV-barrier property and strong antioxidant activity. Moreover, the antibacterial efficacy increased significantly. Therefore, the CD-added gelatin films have great potential as a value-added, low-cost, safe, and functional nanofiller for the preparation of packaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: As the COVID-19 pandemic continues, interest in active packaging with antimicrobial properties is also growing in the food packaging sector. The demand for antimicrobial packaging films increases since antimicrobial packaging can protect food from microbial contamination and prevent food spoilage (Zhang et al., 2021). Recently, scientists have applied various nanomaterials from different sources to develop active films that play an active role in food preservation. As a new functional nanomaterial, quantum dots are used in various fields as an economical and abundant functional material (Cailotto et al., 2018).

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon-based spherical nanoparticles, typically less than 10 nm in size, widely used for non-human contact applications (Levy and Marshall, 2004). Due to photoluminescence and other beneficial properties such as water solubility, biocompatibility, and low toxicity, the CD has also been utilized in human contact biomedical applications such as cell imaging (Anand et al., 2019; Batool et al., 2020). In addition, CD's strong antioxidant and antibacterial activity and low toxicity make it a good candidate for developing functional packaging materials for packaging applications (Chatzimitakos et al., 2020; Moradi et al., 2021).

Until now, an efficient CD synthesis method using a simple carbon source has been developed and is being used in many research fields as an economical and sustainable technology (Cailotto et al., 2018). Various carbon source materials have been used to manufacture CDs, but their properties depend on the raw materials (Kang et al., 2020). According to the Food Waste Index Report (2021), 931 million tonnes of food are wasted worldwide. This food waste, which is being thrown away in large quantities worldwide, can be used as a raw material for CD synthesis, making it a sustainable and high value-added functional material. One interesting potential source of CD is potato skins, often obtained as a by-product of potato processing. The total carbohydrate content of raw potato skins is 8.7–12.4 g/100 g, making it an excellent carbon source. Potato skins also contain free-associated phenolic compounds that provide functional properties along with strong antioxidant activity (Nara et al., 2006). Therefore, if CD is synthesized from potato skins and applied to the production of functional packaging films, it will be possible to contribute to utilizing agricultural by-products and reducing carbon emissions.

Recently, functional films based on biopolymers have been broadly applied for food packaging and preservation due to their biodegradability, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, eco-friendliness, and sustainability (Ezati et al., 2022b; Kumar et al., 2020). Particularly, biopolymer-based food packaging films have excellent biocompatibility and can provide various functions such as antimicrobial properties, so it is expected to be used in various innovative packaging applications instead of plastic packaging films (Jeya Jeevahan et al., 2020). To this end, various polysaccharides, proteins, and lipids from various natural sources are used alone or in combination to manufacture biodegradable packaging materials. Polysaccharides such as starch (de Souza et al., 2020), agar (Mostafavi and Zaeim, 2020), carrageenan (Ezati et al., 2021a), alginate (Chen et al., 2021), and so on have been used to prepare active packaging films. Proteins, like carbohydrates, are renewable natural polymer materials that are abundant in nature and are excellent raw materials for producing biopolymer films. Gelatin is a water-soluble fibrous protein mainly obtained by partially hydrolyzing collagen from wastes generated during animal processing (Mo et al., 2021). It is extensively applied in the food and pharmaceutical industry due to its hydrophilicity, thermoreversible gelling, flexibility, stabilization, texture, and emulsification properties (Jafarzadeh et al., 2021). Gelatin is also interesting due to its excellent film formability, biocompatibility, and biodegradability (Hosseini et al., 2015). However, gelatin films' relatively low UV-barrier property and strength or poor water vapor barrier properties have limited their applications in active packaging films (Qiao et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018a, 2018b). In order to increase the utilization of the gelatin film and give it functionality, it is generally used by reinforcing it with a functional filler.

Several studies to date have individually reported various CDs as multifunctional fillers in food packaging studies. However, no one has used food waste by-products as CD sources in food packaging applications. Therefore, the main goal of this study is to synthesize CDs from potato skins and apply them for the first time as functional ingredients of gelatin-based active packaging films. The CD and CD-incorporated gelatin films were characterized, and the effect of CD on the physicochemical and functional properties of films was studied.

CDs were prepared using a one-step hydrothermal method using potato skin powder. For this, 2 g of potato skin powder were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water with stirring and placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene (Teflon) lined stainless steel reactor (100 mL) and reacted at 200 °C for 6 h in an oil bath. After cooling, the reaction product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to remove unreacted materials. The supernatant was filtered through a membrane filter (25 mm diameter nylon filter, pore size: 0.20 µm, Whatman International Ltd., Maidstone, England). The filtrate was then freeze-dried to obtain a black powder of CD and stored in a refrigerator before further testing. The prepared CDs were characterized using the methods described in the Supporting Information.

Gelatin-based films were prepared using a solution casting method. For this, 4 g of gelatin and 1.2 g of glycerol were dissolved in 150 mL of distilled water and added different amounts of carbon dots (2 wt% and 4 wt% based on the polymer) with vigorous stirring at 80 °C for 30 min. The film-forming solution was immediately cast on the leveled Teflon film (Cole-Parmer Instrument Co., Chicago, IL, USA) coated glass plate (24 cm × 30 cm) and dried at 25 °C for two days. The dried film was peeled off the plate and conditioned at 25 °C and 50% RH for at least 2 days. The film properties were characterized using the methods described in the Supporting Information.

The morphological property of the CD was determined using TEM imaging, and the images are shown in Fig. 1 a-b. The CDs formed a stable suspension without forming aggregation. TEM images showed the formation of nearly spherical CD particles with a uniform particle size of 9.27–9.41 nm, which was in the quantum range as previously reported (Shan et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2017).

The UV/visible light absorption spectra of CD solution and photographs of CD solution under visible and UV light are shown in Fig. 1 c. Due to the quantum confinement effect, the brown color of CD solution turned to an intense green light upon UV excitation, confirming the successful synthesis of CD (Roy et al., 2022). In addition, the CD solution showed a strong light absorption peak at 310 nm due to the n-π * transition of CO or the C-OH bond of CD (Pandiyan et al., 2020). Therefore, it is presumed that the strong UV-absorbing properties of CDs represent high UV-blocking properties, which are essential for UV-blocking packaging applications.

XRD patterns of CD are shown in Fig. 1 d. Two major peaks were observed in the CD around 7 and 22°, consistent with previously reported carbon dots prepared using Trapa bispinosa peel extract (Mewada et al., 2013). The observed peaks are attributed to the (002) and (101) diffraction of graphitic carbon.

According to FTIR spectra of CD (Fig. 1 e), the absorption peaks between 1068 and 960 cm−1 and 616 cm−1 were attributed to stretching C-O/C-C and bending vibrations of aromatic C-H (Fuertes et al., 2010). The absorption bands related to the stretching vibration of O-H (3225 cm−1), CO (1667 cm−1), and a combination of O-C-H/C-O-H vibration (1579 cm−1) and C-H/O-H bonds deformation (1108 cm−1) were observed due to the surface functional groups of the CD (Papaioannou et al., 2018).

For the cytotoxicity, variable concentrations of CD (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 1.0 mg/mL) were cultured in the mouse fibroblast L929 cell line at a fixed density. Cell viability (%) determined after 72 h was concentration-dependent. Cell viability was 99.1%, 91.7%, 93.7%, and 78.8% at CD concentrations of 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, and 1.0 mg/mL, respectively. The cytotoxicity of CD was negligible at the low concentration (0.1 mg/mL), while relatively low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility were observed at the high concentration (1.0 mg/mL). The cytotoxicity of the CD was confirmed by the optical micrographs, showing live fibroblast cells for the control and at a low concentration of CD-treated groups ( Fig. 2). However, it showed a detrimental effect on the growth and proliferation of the fibroblasts after 72 h exposure when treated with a high concentration of CD (> 0.25 μg/mL). Presumably, the cytotoxicity of CDs is attributed to their small size, which is more likely to penetrate the cells and alter cell metabolism, thus changing cellular machinery (Turecka et al., 2018). However, interaction of CD with biological systems depends on several other factors, including electrostatic interaction and surface functional groups (Havrdova et al., 2016). Various cytotoxicity studies have shown that CD (with or without surface modification) exhibits very low toxicity (Havrdova et al., 2016). Moreover, cytotoxicity studies of CD performed to date show that in most cases, concentrations of 10–100 μg/mL cause only a slight loss in cell viability (~10–20%), which is suitable for cell labeling/staining purposes, suggesting their safety for food packaging uses (Ray et al., 2009).

The appearance and surface and cross-sectional FE-SEM images of the gelatin-based films are shown in Fig. 3. The gelatin film was free-standing, colorless, and transparent. However, when CD was added, the film turned brown depending on the CD concentration (Fig. 3 a). In general, the microstructure of composite films is mainly influenced by the compatibility between the filler molecules and the polymer matrix, and the filler distribution within the polymer matrix. The surface morphology of the gelatin/CD films showed a smooth and continuous film structure without cracks or defects (Fig. 3 b). As shown in cross-section images Fig. 3 c, the CDs were uniformly dispersed within the gelatin polymer matrix, indicating that the CDs have high compatibility with the polymer matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FTIR and XRD patterns of films
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectra of the gelatin-based films were analyzed to evaluate the chemical interactions between gelatin and CDs, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 a. In the neat gelatin film, the characteristic peaks of gelatin at 3285, 2937, 1630 cm−1 were observed due to the vibration of O-H, C-H, amide-I of the CO group associated with COO- in the structure. The band around 1542 cm−1 represents the amide-II vibration due to the N-H bending and the C-N stretching (Riahi et al., 2021). The peak at 1235 cm−1 was attributed to the amide-III vibration band, which represented vibration in the plane of C-N and N-H groups of bound amide (Papaioannou et al., 2018). As reported by other researchers, all CD-added gelatin films showed almost identical FTIR spectra in the gelatin film. However, a new peak was appeared at 616 cm−1 by adding CD, which was due to the alkyne C-H bend, indicating that CD was embedded in the film through hydrogen bonding with the carboxylic acid groups of the gelatin.

XRD analysis was performed to study the phase compositions of gelatin-based polymers, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 b. The XRD patterns of the gelatin film exhibited two significant diffraction peaks at 2θ = 7.8° (d101 =11.08 Å) and 22.12° (d101 =4.01 Å), typically allocated to the triple-helical crystalline structure in gelatin (Asma et al., 2014). Compared with the XRD pattern of the neat gelatin film, the gelatin/CD film showed the same diffraction pattern without additional peaks, indicating a homogeneous distribution of CD within the polymer matrix (Kandra and Bajpai, 2020).

The film's mechanical properties significantly impact commercialization as packaging films must meet adequate mechanical strength and integrity to prevent breakage or leakage during food storage and distribution (Priyadarshi et al., 2021a). The mechanical properties of the gelatin-based films are presented in Table 1. The thickness of the gelatin film was significantly increased by the addition of CD, mainly due to the increase in solids. The tensile strength (TS) of the gelatin film was 60.7 MPa, which was relatively strong compared to the carbohydrate-based films such as chitosan, carrageenan, and agar-based films (Ezati and Rhim, 2021a; Roy and Rhim, 2020). When 2 wt% of CD was added to the gelatin film, the TS slightly increased though the increase was not statistically significant (p > 0.05). The flexibility (EB) did not change significantly (p > 0.05), but the stiffness (EM) decreased significantly (p < 0.05). However, the addition of 4 wt% CD significantly decreased the film's TS, EB, and EM. These results indicate that the interfacial interaction and hydrogen bonding between the polymer matrix and the CD increased at low concentrations of CD (Roy et al., 2021), as confirmed by the FT-IR results. On the other hand, when a high concentration of CD was added, CD aggregation occurred and prevented the interaction between polymer chains, thereby reducing the TS of the film (Ezati et al., 2021b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water contact angle (WCA) of films
ce:section.ce:para: The WVP and WCA of the gelatin-based films are also presented in Table 1. The WVP of the film increased significantly (p < 0.05) by the addition of CD (2 and 4 wt%). The CD-added film's decreased water vapor barrier (i.e., increased WVP) may be due to the surface hydrophilic functional groups of the CD (Yadav et al., 2020). Similarly, the WVP of the cellulose nanofiber-based films was found to slightly increase when CD was added (Ezati et al., 2022a).

On the other hand, the WCA of the gelatin-based film was significantly reduced by adding CDs (p < 0.05), perhaps due to increased hydrophilic functional groups of CD. This result confirmed the increased WVP of the CD-added gelatin films. Similar to the present results, the addition of CD to gelatin/carrageenan films has been reported to decrease the hydrophobicity of the films (Roy et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The release rate of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The release of CD from the CD-added gelatin films was evaluated using food simulant solutions, and the results are shown in Fig. 5. The release rate of the CD was dependent on the food simulant solution and the CD concentration. When the gelatin/CD film was immersed in 10% and 50% ethanol, the CD was released rapidly during the first 30 min, then the release rate decreased, and after 1 h, it reached a steady state. On the other hand, the release rates of 95% ethanol solutions are much lower than that of 10% and 50% ethanol solutions. The release rate of CD from gelatin-based films was concentration-dependent. When the concentration of CD increased from 2% to 4%, the release rate in all simulant solutions increased with the increase in the concentration. The release rate is affected by the solubility of the polymer matrix and the affinity between the solvent and the CD. However, alcohols have limited access to gelatin polymers, resulting in CD being trapped in the polymer matrix and reducing diffusion in closed structures (Ezati and Rhim, 2021b). Therefore, CDs were released more slowly in 95% alcohol solutions than 10% and 50% alcohol solutions. The increased release rate of CD is expected to improve the functional properties of the film when it comes into contact with food.

The free radical scavenging ability of the CDs is shown in Fig. 6 a. The CDs showed significant antioxidant activity in ABTS and DPPH methods, dependent on the CD concentration. The high scavenging ability of CD may be due to surface functional groups participating in free radical scavenging to generate more stable products in ABTS and DPPH assays (Zhang et al., 2018a, 2018b). The antioxidant activity determined by the ABTS method was higher than that determined by the DPPH method, which was due to the hydrophilicity of the CD with hydrophilic surface functional groups such as hydroxyl groups (Zhang et al., 2018a, 2018b).

The scavenging ability of the gelatin-based films was assessed by ABTS and DPPH methods. The gelatin film exhibited antioxidant activity of 27.4% and 1.8%, possibly due to some antioxidant peptides in the gelatin (Yang et al., 2008). As shown in Fig. 6 b, the addition of 2% and 4% CD increased the free radical scavenging capacity of the gelatin film to 99.2% and 99.6% in the ABTS method and 72.8% and 93.6% in the DPPH method, respectively. The high antioxidant activity of CD can be attributed to its surface functional groups with free radical scavenging ability (Shan et al., 2014). Similar high antioxidant activity has been observed in CD-added biopolymer-based films such as pectin/gelatin and gelatin/carrageenan films (Ezati et al., 2021b, 2022a; Roy et al., 2021). The free radicals ABTS• and DPPH• can be abridged and reduced by the H• transfer from the hydroxyl groups of the CD to procedure ABTS-H and DPPH-H (Priyadarshi et al., 2021b). The antioxidant activity of the CD-added films was higher as determined by the ABTS than the DPPH method, which may be due to the higher swelling rate of the gelatin film in aqueous ABTS solution than in methanolic DPPH solution (Ezati and Rhim, 2021b). Gelatin/CD film with high antioxidant activity is expected to prevent oxidative degradation of packaged foods vulnerable to oxidative degradation.

The antibacterial efficacy of the CD was qualitatively assessed by the well-diffusion and disk-diffusion methods. As shown in Fig. 7, both methods demonstrate the susceptibility of microorganisms to CD through the size of the inhibition zone, which is facilitated by the solubility, dispersibility, and diffusivity of CDs (Moradi et al., 2019; Travlou et al., 2018). Both well-diffusion and disk-diffusion test results showed that the CD showed no inhibition zone against E. coli, whereas it exhibited significant efficacy on L. monocytogenes, which showed the growth inhibition zone of 0～7 mm and ~6 mm in the well-diffusion and disk-diffusion methods, respectively.

The antibacterial activity of CD can be related to the generation of ROS on the surface of bacteria. ROS, such as O2 -, OH-, and HO2 -, attach to the microbial surface by electrostatic forces, interacting with proteins, lipids, and nucleotides to denature membrane proteins that cause serious cellular physiological damage and ultimately increase permeability and disrupt DNA proliferation associated with cell lysis (Dwyer et al., 2014; Nigam, 2015). The CD-added composite films showed stronger antibacterial activity against L. monocytogenes than E. coli, mainly due to differences in the cell wall structure and affinity for CD interactions (Ezati et al., 2021a, 2022b). Gram-positive bacteria are more susceptible to the harmful effects of ROS, primarily because they lack an outer membrane containing a thick, relatively porous peptidoglycan layer. The cell wall of Gram-negative bacteria has a complex structure in which a relatively thin peptidoglycan layer is sandwiched between two cell membranes composed of phospholipids and lipopolysaccharides on both the inner and outer surfaces. Therefore, the CD cannot directly interact with the peptidoglycan layer because it cannot successfully penetrate the outer cell membrane due to the hydrophobicity of the peptidoglycan layer (Yuan et al., 2021). On the other hand, due to Gram-positive bacteria's simple cell wall structure, the CD can penetrate and cause bacterial infiltration, leading to the degradation of bacteria (Yuan et al., 2021). This results in a permeability barrier to foreign compounds, accordingly relatively weak interactions and preventing CDs from entering bacterial cells, leading to a weak antibacterial effect on Gram-negative bacteria (Travlou et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2019).

This study is aimed to study the applicability of potato skin carbon dots as functional fillers for food packaging films. Potato skin carbon dots (CD) were synthesized by hydrothermal method and used as a functional filler for the gelatin film to enhance its functionality. The CDs were spherical with a 9.27–9.41 nm particle size and showed high antioxidant and antibacterial functions with low cytotoxicity. The addition of CD to the gelatin-based films increased the UV blocking properties without sacrificing the transparency and mechanical properties of the film. Moreover, the addition of CD improved the water vapor barrier and decreased the water-resistance of gelatin film. The CD-added gelatin films showed high antioxidant activity in the DPPH and ABTS methods and a moderate bactericidal effect on L. monocytogenes. Therefore, the CD has a high potential for preparing functional films in active packaging applications to secure food safety.

Seungjae Min: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – draft. Parya Ezati: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Data analysis, Writing – draft. Jong-Whan Rhim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (2022R1A2B02001422).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.indcrop.2022.114820.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
